title: AI UX Porridge - 2026-07-03
description:
Saturday, July fourth, twenty twenty-six. Let's start with the browser wars. They're not about search anymore. Google and Apple still dominate, but 2026 has brought a wave of AI-powered alternatives—Perplexity's Comet, The Browser Company's Dia—all betting the browser becomes less a window onto the web and more an assistant that gets things done. These aren't just incremental updates. They're rethinking what a browser fundamentally is.

Next: Mark Zuckerberg told Meta staff this week that AI agents haven't progressed as fast as he'd hoped. Meta laid off eight thousand people earlier this year and shuffled another seven thousand into AI groups, betting big on agents replacing human work. But the payoff hasn't materialized yet. Zuckerberg said the upside "hasn't come to fruition yet," though he expects improvements in the next three to six months. Meta's spending one hundred forty-five billion on AI infrastructure this year. That's a massive bet that still hasn't paid dividends.

Third: If you design in Figma and wondered how your work actually gets from design into code with AI, a new breakdown walks through the mess. There's no single pipeline yet. The demos show one clean layer working perfectly. Real work needs three or four layers stacked together, and nobody shows you the stack because that's where it gets messy and half-solved. The confusion comes from treating separate things at different stages as one monolithic problem. Understanding the layers—starting with MCPs, the connections between tools—is where clarity starts.

And finally: Good UX might have broken the job market. Ten years ago, about fifteen percent of applications became interviews. Today that's dropped to two or three percent. Why? Friction disappeared. LinkedIn's Easy Apply, one-click applications, resume autofill, AI-generated cover letters—applying now takes thirty seconds instead of thirty minutes. From the applicant's perspective, that's outstanding UX. From the employer's perspective, it's chaos. They're drowning in applications. Better experience for users created a worse outcome for the system.

Thanks for listening. See you tomorrow.

title: AI UX Porridge - 2026-07-02
description:
Thursday, July 2nd, 2026.

Privacy-first AI platform Venice AI just hit unicorn status with a $65 million Series A. The startup gives users access to over 200 AI models while keeping their data encrypted and untracked. They're already profitable with $70 million in annualized revenue and serving 3 million active users.

India's Bhavin Turakhia is betting $30 million of his own money that the AI era demands completely redesigned workplace software. His new company, Neo, combines project management, documents, storage, and AI into one platform—designed from scratch for AI, not bolted on afterward. Turakhia says you can't just add AI to old enterprise tools any more than you could turn Nokia parts into an iPhone.

Cloudflare just issued AI companies a September deadline. Starting September 15th, their default settings will block "mixed-use" web crawlers from ad-supported pages, forcing a separation between search crawlers and AI training crawlers. The move signals that website owners want their content discoverable but compensated—and Cloudflare's calling out Google for having unfair access to more training data through its search dominance.

On the design side: AI personality is emerging as a critical design problem. Right now it's an accidental byproduct of training and alignment, but researchers argue it should be deliberately designed like any other interface element. The warmth, hedging, and eagerness of AI assistants all shape how users feel—yet nobody's actually choosing these traits.

And Google's Gemini Spark agent is now available on Mac, with new integrations for Google Tasks, Keep, Canva, and Instacart. The expansion lets Spark handle multi-step workflows across your files and connected services.

That's your Thursday briefing.
